name: Triton
summary: OpenAI's GPU kernel DSL: Python-embedded language for writing tile-based GPU kernels. Compiles via MLIR using polyhedral techniques to schedule tensor-core operations. Designed to fill the gap between hand-written CUDA (highest performance, hardest to write) and PyTorch operators (easiest, less tunable).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Triton** (OpenAI) is a GPU kernel DSL embedded in Python: programmers write tile-based GPU kernels using Triton's tile primitives, and the Triton compiler emits optimised PTX/SASS via MLIR. The design fills the gap between hand-written CUDA (highest performance, hardest to write) and PyTorch operators (easiest, less tunable).

A Triton kernel looks like a Python function that operates on tiles — sub-blocks of a tensor — rather than individual elements. The compiler handles tile placement, memory hierarchy management, and tensor-core scheduling automatically. Programmers express the algorithm structure; Triton handles the GPU-specific scheduling.

sections.title: Design
sections.content:
Triton's compiler uses polyhedral techniques to schedule tile-level operations on GPU tensor cores. The polyhedral model is well-suited to tile-based GPU kernels because tile loops are typically affine and the data-flow between tiles is statically determinable.

The compilation flow: Triton Python source → Triton MLIR dialect → various optimisation passes → NVPTX (NVIDIA) or AMDGCN (AMD) → PTX/SASS. The MLIR-based intermediate stages let Triton apply optimisations declaratively rather than embedding them in a hand-written backend.

Production usage: most modern PyTorch operators that need custom GPU kernels (`torch.compile`, FlashAttention, fused-MoE kernels) are written in Triton rather than CUDA. The DSL has become the de-facto standard for medium-complexity GPU kernels in the AI/ML ecosystem.
